questions.q: Which Indian scientist discovered the Raman Effect?
questions.explanation: Chandrasekhara Venkata Raman (C. V. Raman) discovered the effect that now bears his name.

questions.q: The Raman Effect involves the scattering of what?
questions.explanation: The Raman Effect is the inelastic scattering of light by molecules.

questions.q: For his discovery, C. V. Raman won which Nobel Prize in 1930?
questions.explanation: C. V. Raman received the 1930 Nobel Prize in Physics for his work on light scattering.

questions.q: C. V. Raman was the first person of which background to win a Nobel Prize in the sciences?
questions.explanation: Raman was the first Asian and first non-white person to receive a Nobel Prize in a scientific field.

questions.q: According to popular accounts, what natural observation inspired Raman to study light scattering?
options: The blue colour of the sea and sky
questions.explanation: Raman was famously inspired by questioning why the Mediterranean Sea appeared deep blue during a voyage.

questions.q: The Raman Effect is widely used today as a tool for what purpose?
questions.explanation: Raman scattering provides a molecular fingerprint, making it a key technique for identifying materials.

questions.q: The analytical technique based on the Raman Effect is called what?
questions.explanation: Raman spectroscopy analyses scattered light to determine molecular structure and composition.

questions.q: In which country was C. V. Raman born?
questions.explanation: C. V. Raman was born in 1888 in Tiruchirappalli (Trichinopoly), in present-day Tamil Nadu, India.

questions.q: National Science Day in India is celebrated each year on 28 February to mark what?
options: The discovery of the Raman Effect
questions.explanation: India celebrates National Science Day on 28 February to commemorate Raman's discovery of the effect in 1928.

questions.q: In the Raman Effect, the scattered light has a frequency that is generally what compared to the incident light?
questions.explanation: Because the scattering is inelastic, a small fraction of scattered light has a shifted (different) frequency.

questions.q: In which year did C. V. Raman announce his discovery of the effect?
questions.explanation: Raman discovered and announced the effect in 1928, with the famous experiment on 28 February.

questions.q: At which institution was C. V. Raman working when he discovered the Raman Effect?
questions.explanation: Raman made the discovery in Calcutta, associated with the University of Calcutta and the Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science (IACS).

questions.q: Raman collaborated closely on the 1928 experiments with which of his students?
questions.explanation: K. S. Krishnan worked with Raman and co-authored the announcement of the new scattering phenomenon.

questions.q: When a molecule gains energy and the scattered photon loses energy (shifts to lower frequency), the band is called what?
questions.explanation: Stokes lines appear at lower frequency because the molecule absorbs some of the photon's energy.

questions.q: Elastic scattering of light, where the frequency does NOT change, is known as what?
questions.explanation: Rayleigh scattering is the dominant, elastic scattering in which the scattered light keeps the same frequency.

questions.q: Roughly what fraction of scattered photons undergo the inelastic Raman scattering?
options: A very tiny fraction (about one in a million)
questions.explanation: Raman scattering is extremely weak, with only about one in a million photons being inelastically scattered.

questions.q: Modern Raman spectroscopy commonly uses which light source to provide intense, monochromatic light?
questions.explanation: Lasers provide the intense monochromatic light needed to make the weak Raman signal practical to measure.

questions.q: The light source Raman originally used for his classic 1928 experiment was concentrated light from what?
questions.explanation: Lasers did not exist in 1928; Raman used filtered sunlight and later mercury arc lamps as monochromatic sources.

questions.q: The shift in frequency observed in Raman scattering corresponds to what property of the molecule?
questions.explanation: The Raman shift equals the energy of molecular vibrational or rotational transitions, giving structural information.

questions.q: Besides the Raman Effect, C. V. Raman is also known for fundamental studies in which field?
questions.explanation: Raman did pioneering work on the acoustics of Indian musical instruments such as the violin, veena and mridangam.

questions.q: Which Indian institute did C. V. Raman lead as director, and where he later founded his own research institute?
questions.explanation: Raman served as director of the Indian Institute of Science in Bangalore and later founded the Raman Research Institute there.

questions.q: What is India's highest civilian award that was conferred on C. V. Raman in 1954?
questions.explanation: C. V. Raman was among the first recipients of the Bharat Ratna, India's highest civilian honour, in 1954.

questions.q: When a molecule is already in an excited state and the scattered photon gains energy (shifts to higher frequency), the resulting band is called what?
questions.explanation: Anti-Stokes lines occur at higher frequency because the molecule transfers vibrational energy to the photon.

questions.q: At ordinary temperatures, why are Stokes lines typically more intense than anti-Stokes lines?
options: Most molecules start in the ground vibrational state (Boltzmann distribution)
Anti-Stokes lines are absorbed by the detector
The laser only produces Stokes photons
questions.explanation: By the Boltzmann distribution, far more molecules occupy the ground state, so Stokes (upward) transitions dominate.

questions.q: A Russian/Soviet pair, Landsberg and Mandelstam, independently observed Raman scattering in 1928 in what material?
questions.explanation: Landsberg and Mandelstam observed the same combinational scattering of light in quartz crystals at nearly the same time.

questions.q: The selection rule for Raman activity requires a change in which molecular property during a vibration?
questions.explanation: A vibration is Raman-active only if it produces a change in the molecule's polarizability.

questions.q: Raman spectroscopy is often described as complementary to which other vibrational spectroscopy technique?
questions.explanation: Raman and IR spectroscopy are complementary because their selection rules (polarizability vs dipole change) differ.

questions.q: The Raman shift, the difference between incident and scattered light, is conventionally expressed in what units?
questions.explanation: Raman shifts are reported in wavenumbers (cm⁻¹) because the shift in this unit is independent of the excitation wavelength.

questions.q: Which enhanced variant of the technique boosts the normally weak Raman signal by adsorbing molecules on roughened metal (e.g. silver or gold) surfaces?
questions.explanation: Surface-Enhanced Raman Spectroscopy (SERS) dramatically amplifies the signal using nanostructured metal surfaces.

questions.q: C. V. Raman shared a noted family scientific legacy: which Nobel laureate astrophysicist was his nephew?
questions.explanation: Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar, who won the 1983 Nobel Prize in Physics, was C. V. Raman's nephew.
